title: Operator Discovery as Denoising
subtitle: A Diffusion Perspective on Wake-Sleep Compression

children.children: What if discovering conceptual primitives is the same process as removing noise from an image?

children: This tutorial explores a deep structural parallel: wake-sleep operator discovery — where messy event descriptions get compressed into clean Schankian primitives — is isomorphic to diffusion-based denoising. Each wake-sleep iteration is a denoising step. The MDL objective is a noise schedule. And the operator library is a learned denoiser.

props.title: 1. The Noise Problem in Commonsense Knowledge

children.children: Consider ATOMIC, a commonsense knowledge base with ~600K event tuples like:
Event: 'PersonX gives PersonY a ride to the airport' → Effect: 'uses gas'
Event: 'PersonX steals from the store' → Effect: 'gets arrested'
Event: 'PersonX teaches PersonY piano' → Effect: 'can play songs'

These are described in natural language — messy, ambiguous, redundant. A regex-based parser can classify some into structured state changes (location_change, possession_gain, etc.), but 77% end up in a 'generic' bucket. That 77% is noise — signal the system can't yet read.

Imagine a photograph taken through frosted glass. You can make out the large shapes (a person, a building) but the fine details are lost in noise. That's what ATOMIC looks like to a regex parser — the big categories (someone moved, someone felt something) are visible, but 77% of the content is just blur. An LLM adapter is like cleaning the glass: suddenly 'uses gas' resolves into a structured state change (resource_consumption), 'gets arrested' becomes (social_status: free → arrested), and 'can play songs' becomes (capability_change: can't play → can play). Zero percent generic. The noise is gone.

props.title: Generic Rate: Regex vs LLM Adapter

data.description: Percentage of ATOMIC events classified as 'generic' (unresolvable) by each parsing method. Lower is better — 0% means every event gets a structured state-change classification.

children.children: Diffusion models generate images by learning to reverse noise. The process has two phases:

children.definition: Start with a clean image. Gradually add Gaussian noise over T steps until you have pure static. Each step adds a little more blur. This is easy — just add random numbers.

Train a neural network to predict and remove the noise at each step. Starting from pure static, apply the denoiser T times to recover a clean image. Each step removes a little blur. This is hard — it requires learning what 'clean' looks like.
The sequence of noise levels σ₁ > σ₂ > ... > σ_T that controls how much noise is present at each step. Early steps have lots of noise (big shapes), late steps have little (fine details).

children.children: The key insight: the noise isn't a bug — it's the medium. You can't skip steps because each step depends on the ambiguity remaining from the previous one. Early steps resolve global structure; late steps resolve local details.
Now watch what happens when we line up the two processes side by side:

rows: Defines what counts as 'resolved' vs 'still noisy'
The knowledge that enables noise removal
External signal that helps resolve ambiguity

props.title: Why This Isn't Just a Metaphor

children.children: The mapping is structural, not just poetic. Both processes:
1. Start from maximum entropy (noise/unstructured events) and converge toward minimum description length (clean image/compressed operators).
2. Are iterative — you can't jump from noise to clean in one step. Each iteration depends on the partial structure recovered so far.

3. Use a 'schedule' that defines the resolution hierarchy — diffusion uses σ_t, operator discovery uses MDL thresholds that determine when a pattern is 'compressed enough' to become a named primitive.
4. Build a reusable model — the diffusion denoiser generalizes to new images; the operator library generalizes to new events.

5. Have a natural curriculum — diffusion resolves global structure before local detail; operator discovery should find coarse-grained operators (PTRANS = 'something moved') before fine-grained ones (PTRANS.walk vs PTRANS.drive).

props.title: 4. The MDL Objective as Noise Schedule

children.children: In diffusion models, the noise schedule σ₁ > σ₂ > ... > σ_T is a hyperparameter that controls the denoising process. In operator discovery, the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle plays exactly this role.

Iteration 1 (high noise): The system sees 'PersonX walks to the store', 'PersonX drives to work', 'PersonX flies to Paris.' All are unstructured text. MDL score: HIGH (each event described individually).
Iteration 2 (medium noise): The system discovers PTRANS — a single operator for 'entity changes location.' Now 'walks to store' = PTRANS(X, store). MDL score: LOWER (many events share one operator).

Iteration 3 (low noise): The system discovers that PTRANS has sub-types: PTRANS.walk (slow, on foot), PTRANS.drive (vehicle), PTRANS.fly (air). MDL score: LOWEST (fine-grained operators capture the remaining structure).

Each iteration reduces the MDL score — exactly like each denoising step reduces the noise level. The process converges when adding more operators would increase MDL (Occam's razor kicks in, like the diffusion model reaching a clean image).

data.description: MDL decreases as operators are discovered (denoising), then plateaus when the signal is fully compressed.

children.children: The parallel goes deeper. Peter Balogh observed that transformers themselves operate like diffusion models:

This reframes the Hydra architecture: Hydra is to transformers what direct generation is to diffusion. Instead of 12 iterative denoising steps over ambiguous representations, Hydra does 3-4 named resolution passes where each one targets a specific dimension of ambiguity (register, phrase structure, thematic roles, information structure). Fewer steps, but each one is surgical rather than diffuse.

props.title: Evidence: Phase 0 Proved the Noise Matters

children.children: In the Hydra Phase 0 experiment, we tried to give GPT-2 'clean' embeddings by freezing all layers except the embedding table and fine-tuning on register-specific corpora (AAVE, legal, literary). The result: GPT-2 resisted. The drift distribution was long-tailed, not bimodal. The model is optimized to process NOISY embeddings through downstream origami.

This is exactly what happens when you try to start a diffusion model from a clean image instead of noise — it doesn't work. The denoising steps expect noise as input. GPT-2's attention layers expect superposition as input. The noise/ambiguity isn't a bug — it's the computational substrate.

props.title: 6. Practical Implications: Noise Schedules for Operator Discovery

children.children: If this analogy holds structurally, it suggests a concrete improvement to wake-sleep operator discovery: add an explicit noise schedule.

children.definition: Wake-sleep discovers operators at all granularities simultaneously. PTRANS and PTRANS.walk might be discovered in the same iteration. This is like running diffusion without a noise schedule — denoising at all scales at once.

Phase 1: Only discover operators with MDL savings > threshold_high (big, coarse operators like PTRANS, ATRANS). Phase 2: Lower the threshold, discover sub-operators (PTRANS.walk, ATRANS.sell). Phase 3: Lower further, discover modifier operators and edge cases. Each phase is a denoising pass at decreasing noise level.

children.children: Unscheduled (current): Iteration 1 might discover PTRANS, emotion_change, and PTRANS.stumble all at once. The fine-grained PTRANS.stumble might interfere with finding the coarse-grained PTRANS.

Scheduled (proposed): Iteration 1 finds only PTRANS (any location change). Iteration 2, knowing PTRANS exists, can now find PTRANS.walk vs PTRANS.drive as refinements. The coarse structure guides the fine-grained discovery.

This is essentially curriculum learning — but with the curriculum derived from information theory (MDL thresholds) rather than hand-designed. The diffusion analogy tells us the curriculum should exist; MDL tells us what it should look like.

props.title: 7. The Unified Diffusion: Tokens → Meanings → Primitives

children.children: The deepest implication: if both transformers AND operator discovery are diffusion processes, maybe the 'correct' architecture for commonsense reasoning denoises at BOTH levels simultaneously:

Imagine developing a photograph in a darkroom. Level 1: the image emerges from chemical noise — shapes become objects (tokens become meanings). Level 2: simultaneously, you're developing a CONTACT SHEET — the individual images organize into a narrative (meanings become primitive story elements). Both denoising processes happen in the same chemical bath, at the same time, each informing the other. A blurry face resolving into 'a person running' (Level 1) simultaneously resolves 'PTRANS is happening' (Level 2). The two levels of denoising are coupled — structure at one level constrains structure at the other.

This is what a Hydra architecture coupled with a lambda knowledge store would do: the heads denoise tokens into structured representations (register, syntax, roles), while the lambda store denoises representations into primitive operators (ATRANS, PTRANS, MTRANS). The register head — the proposed 'Rosetta Stone' between manifolds — is exactly the coupling mechanism between the two denoising levels.

children.term: Diffusion on Syntax Trees (Russell et al., ICLR 2025)
children.definition: Trains a diffusion model over program abstract syntax trees, using a value model to guide denoising toward correct programs. Our operator discovery does the same over operator trees, with MDL as the value function.
The wake-sleep library learning system we build on. Their 'abstraction' sleep phase is the denoising step — extracting reusable program components from solved examples.

Extends DreamCoder with a 'document' phase where an LLM describes discovered abstractions in natural language. This is like adding a semantic classifier to the denoiser — it doesn't just remove noise, it names what's left.

Separates discrete diffusion into a planner (decides what to denoise next) and a denoiser (does the denoising). Our LLM adapter is the planner — it decides which events to resolve — and the wake-sleep loop is the denoiser.

Text generation as diffusion — LLaDA literally denoises text from masked tokens. Dendritic generation (skeleton → fill branches) does explicitly what transformers do implicitly. The circle closes: text diffusion, operator discovery, and transformer processing are all instances of the same denoising process at different scales.

questions.question: In the diffusion-operator discovery parallel, what corresponds to the 'noise' in the diffusion process?
questions.explanation: The 77% of events that a regex parser can't classify are the 'noise' — unresolved signal that needs to be denoised (structured) by better parsing methods.

questions.question: Why is the MDL objective analogous to a noise schedule?
options: Both define what counts as 'resolved' vs 'still noisy' at each step
questions.explanation: The noise schedule σ_t defines the noise level at each diffusion step. The MDL objective defines the compression threshold — below a certain description length, a pattern is 'resolved' (becomes a named operator). Both control the granularity of resolution at each iteration.

questions.question: What does Phase 0 of Hydra reveal about the transformer-diffusion parallel?
options: GPT-2 works better with clean embeddings
GPT-2 resists clean embeddings — it's optimized to process noisy/superposed inputs, just like diffusion models expect noise as input
questions.explanation: When we tried to give GPT-2 clean, register-resolved embeddings (by freezing all layers and fine-tuning only embeddings), the model resisted. It's optimized for 'muddy' inputs — just as a diffusion denoiser expects noisy input and can't operate on clean images.

questions.question: What practical improvement does the diffusion analogy suggest for wake-sleep operator discovery?
options: Add a coarse-to-fine noise schedule: discover big operators first, then refine to sub-operators
Replace wake-sleep with a diffusion model
questions.explanation: The analogy suggests adding an explicit schedule: first discover coarse operators (PTRANS = any movement) with high MDL thresholds, then lower the threshold to find fine-grained sub-operators (PTRANS.walk, PTRANS.drive). This is curriculum learning derived from information theory.
